ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Yellow Dal Tadka; closest parent 'lentil food product' (FOODON_03311434) used as the nearest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Lentils, cooked) is the closest parent; no specific code exists for the tadka-tempered preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 256319004 represents lentil as a food substance; no specific concept for Yellow Dal Tadka exists.

main_ingredients: yellow lentils (toor dal or moong dal)

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of Yellow Dal Tadka is approximately 250 g, served as a bowl of cooked yellow lentils with tempering, usually accompanied by rice or Indian bread.

nutrient_source.title: Dal, cooked (Toor / Arhar dal, cooked) — Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research — National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) for cooked toor dal (arhar dal), which is the primary lentil used in Yellow Dal Tadka. Values reflect the cooked state per 100 g. Tadka (tempering with ghee/oil and spices) adds a modest fat contribution; fat_g has been adjusted upward by approximately 1.5 g per 100 g to account for typical tadka fat addition, consistent with ICMR-NIN composite dish methodology. Sodium reflects typical restaurant-level salting.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Lentils, red, boiled (GI=29)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: Yellow dal tadka is prepared from yellow lentils (toor or moong dal). The University of Sydney GI Database and the 2021 International GI Tables (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care) report GI of 29 for red/yellow lentils boiled. This is used as the closest analogous estimate. Tadka tempering adds fat which may further lower the effective GI. GL per serving calculated as: (29 × 12.5 × 250) / (100 × 100) = 9.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from restaurant-level salting and tadka seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Restaurant-prepared Yellow Dal Tadka may contain 300–400 mg sodium per 100 g; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and limit portion size to one bowl per meal to keep sodium intake within daily targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI 29) and high dietary fibre content (3.8 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Yellow Dal Tadka has a low GI of approximately 29 and provides substantial dietary fibre, resulting in a moderate glycemic load per serving; it is a suitable legume-based protein source for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without large quantities of refined bread or rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High soluble fibre and plant protein with moderate saturated fat from ghee tadka
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Yellow Dal Tadka provides heart-protective soluble fibre and plant protein; however, ghee-based tadka contributes saturated fat (~1.1 g per 100 g), so individuals with cardiovascular disease should prefer oil-based tadka and monitor overall saturated fat intake across the meal.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yellow Dal Tadka is a classic North Indian cooked lentil dish made from yellow lentils — most commonly toor dal (split pigeon peas) or moong dal (split mung beans) — that are pressure-cooked or simmered until soft and then finished with a hot tempering (tadka) of ghee or oil infused with cumin seeds, mustard seeds, dried red chillies, garlic, ginger, onion, and tomato. The tadka is poured sizzling over the cooked lentils just before serving, imparting a smoky, aromatic depth. The dish has a smooth, slightly thick consistency with a mildly spiced, earthy, and savoury flavour profile. It is a staple of everyday Indian home cooking and is widely served in Indian restaurants across Singapore, including at establishments such as Mr Tandoor and Manam. Nutritionally, Yellow Dal Tadka is a valuable source of plant-based protein (approximately 6.8 g per 100 g), dietary fibre (3.8 g per 100 g), folate, iron, and potassium. Its low glycemic index (approximately 29) makes it particularly suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or seeking sustained energy release. The dish is naturally gluten-free and vegetarian, though it is not vegan when prepared with ghee. Clinically, the high fibre content supports glycaemic control and cardiovascular health, while the sodium content in restaurant preparations warrants attention for individuals with hypertension. It is typically served with steamed basmati rice, jeera rice, naan, or roti.
common_user_queries: Is dal tadka good for diabetics?
How many calories in dal tadka?

meal_context: side dish with rice or bread

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods for cooked toor dal, which is the primary lentil used in this dish. The adaptation accounts for tadka fat addition using ICMR-NIN composite dish methodology. No exact entry for 'Yellow Dal Tadka as served in Singapore' exists in any approved primary source; hence confidence is medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN (2017) cooked toor dal data with a modest upward adjustment to fat_g (~+1.5 g per 100 g) to account for ghee/oil tadka, consistent with ICMR-NIN composite dish methodology. 2. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): this is a plant-based legume dish with no animal-derived ingredients except optional ghee; vitamin B12 is naturally zero in plant foods and confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN lentil data. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category. 4. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source database for this food category. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero): no hydrogenated fats are used in traditional dal tadka preparation; confirmed negligible per ICMR-NIN. 6. GI of 29 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database / 2021 International GI Tables for boiled yellow/red lentils as the closest analogous food; tadka fat addition may further reduce effective GI. 7. Sodium of 310 mg per 100 g reflects typical restaurant-level salting; home-prepared versions may have significantly lower sodium. 8. food_group retained as 'Meat and alternatives' from input, consistent with legume classification as a protein-rich plant food alternative. 9. The input cuisine_tags included 'Butter Chicken' and 'Biryani' which are restaurant category tags from field survey, not attributes of this dish; these have been excluded from the Veritas record.
